content.text: Based on the resources from Clawdbot/OpenClaw, AgentMail, and FAUN.dev,
event-driven architecture where an AI agent automatically processes incoming emails, classifies them, drafts responses, and helps maintain a zero-inbox
—turning email management into an automated workflow.

attrs.alt: Email-triggered agents (Clawdbot inbox clearing pattern) concept diagram

content.text: automated email triage and response system
using OpenClaw (formerly Clawdbot) with Gmail Pub/Sub skills:
New Email → Gmail Pub/Sub → Clawdbot Agent → Classify + Draft Response → Inbox Cleared

1. Email arrives in inbox
   ↓
2. Gmail Pub/Sub triggers webhook to Clawdbot
   ↓
3. Agent reads email (subject + body)
   ↓
4. Agent classifies category (urgent, action required, feedback, spam, etc.)
   ↓
5. Agent tags with label (Billing / Bug / Sales / Ops / etc.)
   ↓
6. Agent drafts response for human review
   ↓
7. Agent generates daily summary for managers
   ↓
8. Inbox stays organized until resolved
AgentMail is an API-first email platform designed specifically for AI agents
Isolated by default, created instantly through API
Pay per email, not monthly subscription
Give Clawdbot its own dedicated inbox, completely separate from your personal email

# Install Python SDK
pip install agentmail python-dotenv

# Set environment variable
export AGENTMAIL_API_KEY=your_key_here

:
- Read emails via Gmail Pub/Sub skills
- Classify by sender, subject, and content analysis
- Generate daily morning summary for managers
- Draft responses for human review
- Manage zero-inbox through triggered actions

Gmail inbox / AgentMail inbox
    ↓
Pub/Sub webhook triggers Clawdbot
: Use your personal Gmail with Pub/Sub
: Give Clawdbot dedicated AgentMail inbox (recommended for security)

One of them was I told it to read all the emails we receive at Orai to their ORI support and I told it to start triaging and it went through everything.
It came up with a few email categories on what to organize it in.
And then it actually did it.
So, it added triage labels to all of them.
So, billing, refund, cancellations, bug, feedback, enterprise, demo request, security, ops, and spam.
Step 3: Deep Reading (Not Just Subject Lines)

And then I noticed that when I went into the feedback, many of those emails were actually in some of the other categories.
So I went back and I said, "Hey, look at the stuff and re-triaged."
So it was just looking at the subject heading and I told it to read the body as well.
So it read the body of the emails and then lo and behold reclassified from feedback.
It found 31 more threads that were like refund, cancellations.
: Read the actual email body, not just subject lines, for accurate classification
Agent generates draft responses for human review
: Human reviews and sends drafts, not auto-send
AI agent generates daily morning summary for managers, highlighting emails that require attention

of manual triage time
- Keep everything in Inbox until resolved

# Create account at console.agentmail.to
# Generate API key in dashboard

pip install agentmail python-dotenv
export AGENTMAIL_API_KEY=your_key_here

# Register webhook endpoint
webhook = client.webhooks.create(
    path="/agentmail",
    events=["message.created"]
)

:
- Navigate to Gmail API Console
- Enable Pub/Sub for inbox
- Create webhook endpoint
Meta security researcher's AI agent accidentally deleted her emails

Summer Yue instructed OpenClaw: "Check inbox, suggest what you would archive or delete, don't act until I tell you to."
    ↓
Personal inbox was too large → triggered compaction
    ↓
Compaction lost original instruction
    ↓
Agent speed-deleted entire inbox

:
- "humbles like telling OpenClaw 'confirm before' and it speed deleting your inbox"
- "Inbox too large and initiated compaction process, which caused it to disregard original instructions"
Irreversible actions must pass an approval gate BEFORE they run, not rely on stop mechanism afterward

1. Log every destructive action intent before execution
    ↓
2. Insert clear approval checkpoint for any irreversible operation
    ↓
3. Treat 'stop' as command to halt NEXT action, not to undo one already in progress

(1) Log every destructive action intent before execution
(2) Insert clear approval checkpoint for any irreversible operation
(3) Treat 'stop' as command to halt NEXT action, not undo one already in progress
Flag email content as untrusted input in prompts
System prompts treat email requests as suggestions, not commands

When email arrives:
1. Clawdbot reads email (subject + body)
2. Classifies category (urgent, action required, feedback, spam)
3. Adds triage label (Billing, Bug, Sales, Ops, etc.)
4. Drafts response for human review
5. Generates daily summary highlighting emails requiring attention
6. Keeps everything in Inbox until resolved
Keep emails in Inbox until resolved
More accurate classification (e.g., feedback emails that are actually billing)

: emails arrive at the door (inbox), the assistant (Clawdbot) sorts them into labeled bins (categories), writes draft replies for you to review, and gives you a daily summary of what needs attention. But the assistant never throws away anything without your approval — it's your inbox, not theirs.
Pattern: Email → Gmail Pub/Sub → Clawdbot → Classify + Draft Response → Zero Inbox
Uses OpenClaw with Gmail Pub/Sub skills to read emails, draft responses, manage zero-inbox
Classifies by sender, subject, content: urgent, action required, for reference, spam
Label system: Billing / Cancellation / Bugs / Sales / Ops / Feedback / Enterprise / Demo / Security / Spam
Read email body, not just subject
: Give Clawdbot its own dedicated inbox (AgentMail), separate from personal email
: Irreversible actions need approval gate BEFORE execution, not stop after
: Large inboxes trigger compaction, which can lose original "don't act" instructions
Defense layers: Allowlist trusted senders, isolated session for review, untrusted markers, agent training
Benefits: Saved 4 hours of manual triage, daily summary for managers, keeps inbox organized

Irreversible actions (delete, archive) must pass approval gate before they run — the blast-radius problem is architectural, not conversational. Always log destructive intent, get approval checkpoint, and treat 'stop' as halt next action only.
When working with email-triggered agents (clawdbot inbox clearing pattern), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with email-triggered agents (clawdbot inbox clearing pattern). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of email-triggered agents (clawdbot inbox clearing pattern) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
